explanation: This is a simple infographic showing a world map with 5 color-coded continents/regions, each labeled with a single number and category name. The layout is clean with minimal visual elements, ample white space, and straightforward data encoding. The cognitive demand is very low with only 5 data points to process.

This is a horizontal stacked bar chart with 19 categories and three color-encoded income levels per bar. The number of categories is substantial, creating moderate density, and the stacked encoding requires some cognitive effort to compare segments across bars. However, it uses a single chart type with a clear layout and readable labels, keeping it from being highly complex.

This is a horizontal stacked/grouped bar chart with 20 countries and two color-encoded categories (Deceased and Living donors). While the number of data elements is moderate and the dual-encoding adds some complexity, the layout is clean and straightforward with clear labels and a simple legend. The consistent structure and single chart type keep complexity at a moderate level.

This is a grouped bar chart with two categories (refinance and home purchase) across 12 years. While it has a moderate number of bars (~24), the design is clean with only two colors, clear labels, and a straightforward layout. The annotation callouts for 2011 add slight complexity but overall it remains a standard, well-organized bar chart.

This is a scatterplot with 16 labeled data points encoded by four color categories (Dense, Octree, JIT, Opt.) across varying sizes. While each point has a text label adding some visual clutter, the overall layout is straightforward with a single panel, two axes, and moderate density. The labeled points and color encoding add moderate complexity beyond a basic scatterplot.

This is a scatter plot with three color-encoded clusters (red, green, blue) containing roughly 100-150 data points total. The visualization has no axes, labels, legends, or gridlines, which reduces complexity. However, the moderate number of scattered points with some overlap and the three distinct clusters add moderate perceptual demand.

This is a parallel sets/alluvial diagram showing relationships between three categorical variables (Class, Sex, Survived) with two categories each. The crossing ribbons with color encoding create moderate visual complexity through overlapping flows and color mixing. While the structure has only 3 levels with 2 categories each, the intersecting colored bands and their varying widths add perceptual complexity beyond a simple chart.

This is a stacked area chart with 8 categories (countries) over 12 time points. The multiple overlapping shaded areas with similar brown/tan color palette create moderate visual complexity, requiring effort to distinguish individual country contributions. However, it is a single chart with a straightforward layout and clear legend, keeping it from being highly complex.

This is a dual-line time series chart showing two financial instruments (10-year Treasury note and 10-year TIPS) over roughly 3 years. The lines themselves are fairly detailed and jagged, showing daily price movements. Additional annotations including colored callout boxes, a shaded spread area, and descriptive text add moderate complexity beyond a basic line chart, but it remains a single-panel visualization with only two data series.

This is a biplot/PCA-style scatterplot with multiple encoding channels: point color (continuous gradient from black to orange representing values 50-160), point size variation, directional arrows for variable loadings (Fat, Sugar, Calories, Sodium), a regression/axis line with tick marks and numeric labels, dotted reference circles, and labeled outlier points. The moderate number of data points (~60-70) combined with overlapping elements, multiple text annotations, and the color legend create substantial but not extreme visual complexity.

This image shows concentric circular rings radiating from a center point, with a limited color palette of dark green and dark red/maroon. While the rings create some visual pattern, the overall structure is simple and repetitive with no text, labels, axes, or multiple data encodings. The perceptual demand is low due to the uniform, symmetric pattern.

This visualization shows two side-by-side 3D rendered cardiac surfaces with color mapping (orange-yellow gradient) and wireframe mesh overlays. The right panel adds scattered marker points (green diamonds), increasing its complexity. While the 3D surfaces with mesh grids and color encoding add moderate detail, the overall layout is relatively straightforward with just two panels and no dense data encodings or legends.

This is a filled contour plot (spectral function or dynamic structure factor) with approximately 12 color levels, multiple overlaid contour lines creating intricate nested shapes, and two overlaid curves (cyan lines). The dual axes (k and k/k_F, E and E/E_F) add some complexity. The contour structure is moderately dense with complex topology, particularly in the lower-left region where many contour lines converge, but it remains a single-panel visualization with a straightforward color legend.

This visualization combines multiple encoding channels: a continuous color field (heatmap) representing displacement values, overlaid vector arrows showing direction and magnitude, and contour lines delineating regions of equal displacement. The color gradient spans the full spectrum from red to purple, and the spatial patterns are irregular and complex. The combination of three simultaneous visual encodings (color, vectors, contours) on a single panel creates substantial perceptual demand.

This is a dense multi-panel grid of approximately 54 small heatmap matrices (6 rows × 9 columns), each containing detailed pixel-level patterns in purple and green. The repetitive but varied structure across panels, combined with the fine-grained matrix patterns within each cell and multiple labeled column headers, creates high perceptual and cognitive demand. The sheer number of panels and the intricate internal structure of each heatmap push this well into the high complexity range.

This is a highly detailed scientific visualization, likely of a fluid dynamics simulation or astrophysical phenomenon, showing a complex vortex structure with multiple color encodings (blue, red, yellow, green) representing different data variables. The image contains dense particle/glyph distributions, swirling flow patterns, and numerous small-scale features that demand significant perceptual effort to parse. The multi-encoding nature with vector glyphs overlaid on a color-mapped surface adds substantial visual complexity.

This is an extremely dense 3D visualization showing hundreds of rectangular blocks/buildings in a cityscape-like arrangement viewed from above at an angle. The sheer number of overlapping geometric elements, varying heights, shadows, and the lack of any whitespace or labels creates very high visual complexity. The monochromatic grayscale palette with strong contrast between light surfaces and dark shadows adds to the perceptual density.

This is a dense network graph with hundreds of nodes and edges spread across the entire canvas, forming numerous interconnected clusters and subgraphs. The high number of overlapping edges, varying node sizes, and multiple annotated subgroups (SG1-SG4) add significant perceptual complexity. The overall density and intricacy of the network structure demands considerable cognitive effort to parse.

This is a 3D scientific visualization showing flow fields with streamlines, vector glyphs (arrows in multiple colors), and isosurfaces within a rectangular domain. The multiple vortex structures, overlapping streamlines, scattered directional arrows in various colors (red, blue, green, yellow), and 3D perspective create significant perceptual complexity. The multi-encoding nature with simultaneous display of flow topology, direction, and magnitude across four repeating structures demands considerable cognitive effort to interpret.

This is a simple bar chart with only 3 bars, hatched fill pattern, error bars, and minimal labeling. The layout is clean and straightforward with a single variable on each axis, making it very low in visual complexity.

This is an extremely dense treemap or space-filling visualization with hundreds of nested rectangular elements of varying sizes. The image contains multiple levels of hierarchical detail with fine-grained texture throughout, color encoding (green, cyan, gray), and progressively smaller elements toward the bottom creating very high information density. The sheer number of visible elements and the multi-scale structure demand significant perceptual effort to parse.

This is a single bar chart with approximately 18 bars showing percentage change across countries. While it has a moderate number of categories with long rotated labels, it uses only one color and a single data encoding (bar height). The layout is straightforward and easy to parse, placing it in the moderate-low complexity range.

This is a simple dual-line chart with only two lines over 11 time points. The layout is clean with minimal visual elements — just two clearly differentiated lines, basic axis labels, a simple legend, and two endpoint annotations. The overall perceptual and cognitive demand is low.

This is a simple histogram/bar chart showing a right-skewed distribution with approximately 15-18 bars. It uses a single color (black), has minimal labeling (partially illegible axis labels), and no gridlines or additional visual encodings. The chart is straightforward with a single data series and clear visual pattern.

This image shows repeated text ('IEEE Information Visualization 2005') arranged along curved paths at decreasing sizes, with red dots marking endpoints. The visual elements are relatively simple — text along arcs with minimal data encoding. There's some visual interest from the varying scales and curved text paths, but overall the complexity is low with limited data density and few distinct visual elements.

This is a single donut/pie chart with 6 segments, each clearly labeled with percentages. The design is clean with a central label showing total capacity. The accompanying text adds some context but the visualization itself is straightforward with minimal visual complexity.

This is a stacked bar chart with three color-encoded categories across approximately 30 bars. The chart has moderate complexity due to the number of bars and the stacked encoding, but the layout is straightforward with a single panel, clear legend, and standard axes. The varying bar heights create visual interest but the overall structure is a standard chart type.

This is a stacked area chart with three categories spanning about 30 years of data. While the overlapping areas and jagged peaks create moderate visual complexity, it remains a single-panel chart with a clear legend and straightforward encoding. The three layers with varying peaks add some perceptual demand but the overall structure is standard.

This is a matrix-style chart with 5 columns and 17 rows, using three color-coded levels (high, medium, low) to indicate research focus across different cognitive frameworks. The numerous categories and multiple columns create moderate-to-high complexity, requiring systematic scanning to compare patterns across dimensions. The color encoding is simple but the sheer number of cells (~85) and the need to cross-reference rows and columns adds significant cognitive load.

This visualization contains two side-by-side representations of the Netherlands: a choropleth map with numbered regions (provinces) using distinct colors, and a gridded/tile map version showing the same data at a finer granularity with many small colored squares. The dual-view layout, numerous color categories, numeric labels, and the dense grid representation on the right create substantial visual complexity, though the overall layout remains relatively organized.

This visualization contains numerous elliptical glyphs scattered across the canvas with varying orientations, sizes, and color intensities encoding a continuous variable (1.10–4.96). The spatial distribution is moderately dense with overlapping elements, blue rectangular annotations, and dashed connector lines adding additional visual layers. The combination of multi-encoded glyphs, color gradient legend, and annotation overlays creates substantial perceptual complexity.

This image contains a large grid of approximately 60+ small 3D scientific visualization thumbnails arranged in rows and columns against a black background. Each element shows what appears to be a similar 3D rendering with green and orange/red coloring, varying slightly in orientation and size. The repetitive multi-panel layout with numerous individual elements creates substantial visual density and cognitive demand, though the individual elements are relatively simple and similar to each other.

This is a complex network/graph diagram with numerous nodes (circles and squares) connected by many orthogonal edges that cross and overlap throughout the layout. The density of connections, the variety of node types, and the intricate routing of edges across the full canvas create significant perceptual and cognitive demand. While it uses only grayscale and simple geometric shapes, the sheer number of elements and their interconnections push complexity well above moderate levels.

This visualization consists of three similar heatmap panels showing data for years 1999, 2003, and 2005. Each panel has a grid structure with 5 rows (labeled 2-6) and uses a blue color gradient encoding. While there are three panels, the overall structure is repetitive and relatively simple, with a clear two-tone pattern (white/light on left, blue shades on right) and minimal labeling.

This is a timeline/event chart with 11 rows (users) showing multiple event types encoded by color and shape along a time axis. The chart has moderate complexity with numerous overlapping data points across multiple categories (7 in the legend), requiring careful discrimination of colors and shapes. While the layout is straightforward (parallel timelines), the density of markers and multi-encoding scheme adds meaningful perceptual demand.

This is a geospatial visualization with high complexity: it overlays dozens of color-coded and size-coded circles on a detailed topographic relief map of the Himalayan region. Multiple encoding channels are used simultaneously (color for elevation difference trend, size for standard error, position for geography). Additional layers include river basins, major rivers, sub-region boundaries, ICESat glacier tracks, country labels, and statistical annotations, creating substantial perceptual and cognitive demand.

This is a complex multi-row genomic landscape plot (oncoprint-style) with numerous rows representing clinical variables, subtypes, copy number alterations, and somatic mutations across many patient samples (columns). It uses multiple color encodings for sex, age, histology, gains/losses, and mutation types, creating a dense matrix of information. The combination of many categorical rows, dozens of sample columns, and a detailed legend with multiple encoding schemes makes this a high-complexity visualization.

This is a phylogenetic tree/timeline diagram with moderate-to-high complexity. It combines a cladogram with geological time periods, multiple labeled taxa, shaded regions indicating gaps (Romer's gap, Hexapoda gap), an embedded fossil photograph, and a detailed stratigraphic chart at the bottom with numerous geological stage names. The mix of dashed and solid lines, multiple encodings, and dense labeling in the timeline contribute to its complexity.

This visualization combines multiple coordinated views: a temporal stream graph at the top showing topic evolution over time with labeled themes (Protest Activity, Corruption, Income Inequality), and two large word clouds below showing term frequencies for different time periods. The word clouds contain dozens of terms at varying sizes, and the stream graph has multiple overlapping colored bands with annotations. The multi-component layout, dense text, and combined encoding types create substantial visual and cognitive complexity.

This is a highly complex thematic map of Jerusalem showing sound segmentation with multiple overlapping translucent circular elements representing different sound types (police sirens, church bells, call to prayer, etc.) and their combinations. The extensive legend with 16+ categories, dense overlapping of colored concentric circles across the map, street labels, landmark annotations, and the multi-encoding color scheme all contribute to significant perceptual and cognitive demand. The layering of semi-transparent elements creates visual density that requires careful parsing.

This is a dense finite element mesh visualization with thousands of triangular elements colored by a scalar field (0-18 scale). The mesh varies in density with extremely fine elements in the upper region and coarser elements below, creating high visual intricacy. The combination of dense tessellation, continuous color mapping across many elements, and geometric complexity with cutout features makes this perceptually demanding.

This is a highly complex network visualization featuring multiple dense graph clusters with hundreds of nodes and edges, connected by chain-like structures. The image includes detail-on-demand zoom panels (shown as expanding gray triangles from dashed blue circles), multiple color encodings (red, orange, yellow, dark blue nodes), and heavily interconnected subgraphs with near-complete edge density. The multi-level representation with overview and detail views adds significant perceptual complexity.

This is an extremely dense infographic showing Olympic medal data across many countries and many years, with color-encoded bars spanning a large temporal range. There are well over 100 rows (countries) and dozens of columns (Olympic years), creating a massive matrix of small colored rectangles. The dark background with multiple color encodings (pink, magenta, blue, gray) and tiny text labels adds significant perceptual complexity.

This is a highly complex network/graph visualization with hundreds of nodes and dense interconnecting edges. The layout shows hierarchical tree-like structures combined with many cross-links creating significant visual clutter, especially in the central and left portions. The overlapping edges and dense node clusters demand high perceptual and cognitive effort to parse.

This visualization is highly complex, combining multiple coordinated views including a network/flow diagram on the left with colored connection lines of varying thickness, word clouds in the center, and enlarged word cloud panels on the right. There are numerous text elements with varying sizes, colors encoding part-of-speech categories (11 categories in the legend), and dense overlapping connections between clusters. The multi-panel layout with intricate linking patterns and color-coded elements creates substantial perceptual and cognitive demand.

This is a complex multi-panel visualization system with at least 7 coordinated views (labeled A through G), including a base text panel, overview, page view, line view, and word view. Each panel contains dense matrix-like encodings with color-coded cells, grid patterns, and multiple data dimensions. The overall layout is information-dense with numerous small visual elements, text labels, and interconnected components requiring significant cognitive effort to parse.
